title: Trump's plan to redesign every .gov website leads to AI-designed horrors
gist: Trump's National Design Studio, tasked with AI-redesigning 27,000 .gov sites in three years, has produced meager, inconsistent, and accessibility-failing output while facing agency resistance, privacy/surveillance concerns over commercial trackers, and the quiet dropping of its USWDS-standards mandate.

insights.claim: The National Design Studio was tasked by August 2025 executive order to create new standards and overhaul 27,000 dot-gov websites in three years, but about a year in has launched only a few dozen sites, most consisting of a single sign-up page.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Wildly under-resourced government-modernization mandates predictably stall; a cautionary case for AI-accelerated transformation timelines.
insights.quote: Most of the few dozen websites NDS has launched consist of a single page, where visitors can do little more than fill out a sign-up form.

insights.claim: DOGE's cuts dismantled the 18F technology unit and restructured the US Digital Service, and reduced the USWDS team to one full-time employee, gutting the institutional capacity NDS now needs.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Cutting the teams that maintained web standards directly undermined the successor initiative; institutional-knowledge loss is a durable drag.
insights.quote: the USWDS team—which was created in 2015 to ensure government websites were accessible and mobile-friendly—was reduced to one full-time employee after Trump took office
insights.locator: Those teams knew exactly how hard

insights.claim: Only 30 percent of government websites used USWDS standards as of mid-2023 despite years of effort, illustrating how hard agency adoption of web standards is even before NDS.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Baseline adoption friction means top-down redesign mandates face structural resistance regardless of tooling.
insights.quote: only 30 percent of government websites used them as of mid-2023
insights.locator: Those teams knew exactly how hard

insights.claim: NDS's reliance on AI produced visible failures including a TrumpRX.gov image of a child with six toes running toward a starless American flag, and a CIO.gov design pulled after critics found it inaccessible and accidentally exposed.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Unsupervised AI-generated government content produces reputationally damaging errors; human oversight remains non-negotiable at scale.
insights.quote: showed a child with six toes running towards an American flag without any stars on it

insights.claim: One NDS single-page site shipped close to three megabytes of code, failing basic ADA compliance—a payload critic Ethan Marcotte called comically outsized for a single page of one text style.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Bloated AI-generated code creates real accessibility and mobile-performance liabilities and potential legal exposure under ADA.
insights.quote: ships close to three megabytes of code to boot
insights.locator: One former federal designer, Ethan Marcotte

insights.claim: Sloppy AI-generated code on government sites risks introducing cybersecurity vulnerabilities, not just embarrassing images, according to former government workers who say AI could work only with careful monitoring.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: AI-authored public-sector code is a live security-risk surface; a vector worth watching for breaches and vendor scrutiny.
insights.quote: sloppy AI code could also introduce cybersecurity risks
insights.locator: Without that oversight, government sites risk

insights.claim: Four NDS-built federal sites (ndstudio.gov, trumprx.gov, realfood.gov, trumpaccounts.gov) ran commercial visitor-tracking software configured to evade users' privacy tools, without the public filings required by the Privacy Act of 1974 and E-Government Act of 2002.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Undisclosed commercial trackers on .gov sites raise legal and data-governance red flags; the trackers were removed only after press contact.
insights.quote: run “commercial visitor-tracking software” that’s “configured to evade the privacy tools many web users install.”

insights.claim: NDS built unauthorized versions of services legally assigned to other agencies, including a vote.gov design requiring identity verification via Login.gov and citizenship checks against a DHS database, with no privacy impact assessment.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Centralizing sensitive voter/citizenship data in the White House without assessment is a major civil-liberties and legal-challenge risk.
insights.quote: voters would be required to verify their identity through Login.gov, the federal sign-in gateway, and to have their citizenship checked against a database run by the Department of Homeland Security

insights.claim: Unlike DOGE, NDS has no power to compel agencies to update sites, and as scrutiny intensified most agencies are now resisting engagement, with none responding to USWDS outreach about Trump's July 4, 2026 deadline.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Without enforcement authority, top-down design mandates collapse into voluntary participation—predicting the initiative stalls.
insights.quote: NDS appears largely powerless to direct agencies to update their sites
insights.locator: NDS can’t force agencies to update sites

insights.claim: The requirement that NDS revise the USWDS standards was quietly dropped, at least temporarily, from Trump's executive order, removing the core mechanism meant to unify government web design.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Dropping the standards mandate signals the unification goal is effectively abandoned; the whole redesign premise is in doubt.
insights.quote: the section of Trump’s order requiring updates to USWDS is “no longer a requirement.”
insights.locator: A message posted in the USWDS public Slack

insights.claim: The Guardian found no record of the National Design Studio in USAspending as either a paying agency or a fund recipient, leaving its oversight and funding unaccounted for.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Opaque funding for a data-collecting White House entity compounds accountability concerns and invites investigation.
insights.quote: a search of USAspending returns no record of the National Design Studio either as a paying agency or as a recipient of funds
insights.locator: Following its investigation, the Guardian concluded

insights.claim: Airbnb cofounder Joe Gebbia, serving as Trump's chief design officer, wanted government websites to feel like 'an Apple Store-like experience' and to 'delight' Americans, an aesthetic ambition contradicted by inconsistent fonts and traditional-looking output across NDS sites.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Silicon Valley polish rhetoric collides with public-sector constraints; a template for why private-design leaders struggle in government.
insights.quote: he told Fox News he wanted visiting government websites to feel like “an Apple Store-like experience.”

insights.claim: NDS reportedly plans to use Salesforce AI tools to personalize government websites with real-time and historical data and AI-driven content recommendations, such as dynamically adapting VA.gov to veterans' prior interactions.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Salesforce AI personalization on .gov sites is both a commercial-vendor opportunity and a usability/privacy risk to watch.
insights.quote: creating tailored web experiences using real-time and historical data with AI-driven content and product recommendations
insights.locator: The design studio also reportedly plans

insights.claim: NDS's current top priority appears to be recruiting a two-year, White House-backed engineering 'Tech Force,' but former workers bruised by DOGE cuts are unlikely to join entities that dissolve in two years.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Short-lived, reputation-damaged programs struggle to attract talent, further limiting execution capacity.
insights.quote: a two-year, White House–backed engineering corps
insights.locator: Right now, NDS’s top priority appears to be recruiting
